references.notes: The poet explicitly states an oath ('حلف امرئ') sworn by 'هدية' (votive offering) and 'مقام' (sacred standing place), which are pre-Islamic sacred entities or ritual practices.
The phrase 'والله' is an explicit oath, commonly used in pre-Islamic Arabia to swear by the supreme deity.
'الدهر' (Time/Fate) is invoked as a powerful, impersonal force, reflecting a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The phrase 'وراء البيت' (behind the House) refers to the Kaaba, a central pre-Islamic sanctuary and sacred space.
The term 'تَاللَهِ' (By God) is an explicit pre-Islamic oath formula invoking a deity.
The phrase 'حتفاً بمقدار' (death by measure/fate) implies a concept of predetermined destiny or fate, a common pre-Islamic cosmological idea.
The term 'حدثان الدهر' (the events/calamities of Time/Fate) refers to 'Dahr' as a cosmological force responsible for events, a key pre-Islamic belief.
The mention of 'حُمساً' (Ḥums) refers to a specific group of pre-Islamic Arabs, primarily the Quraysh, who observed distinct ritual practices related to the pilgrimage and sacred spaces.
The term 'maniyya' (fate/appointed death) is used in these verses to describe an irresistible force, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of destiny.
The verse describes a 'mujnaʾa' (grave) covered with heavy stones and wood, which is an explicit reference to pre-Islamic burial practices.
The personal name 'ʿAbd Shams' (Servant of Shams) explicitly refers to Shams, the pre-Islamic sun deity, indicating a form of devotion or acknowledgment.
This verse describes a woman lamenting ('تنوح'), which was a significant ritualistic aspect of pre-Islamic mourning practices for the deceased.

The phrase 'ربابة أيسار' explicitly refers to the bundle of arrows used in the pre-Islamic practice of *maysir*, which involved casting lots for divination or ritualistic distribution of meat, often with marked arrows ('وشوم').
This verse explicitly refers to 'pilgrims with matted hair' (الحجيج الملبد) crying out, which is a direct allusion to the pre-Islamic Hajj pilgrimage and the ritual state of ihram.
The phrase 'أرى الدهر' (I see Time/Dahr) refers to Dahr as a powerful, active cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic belief about fate and the nature of existence.
This verse explicitly mentions the act of sacrifice (naḥr) performed at al-Khayf, a sacred location in Mina associated with pre-Islamic pilgrimage rituals.
This verse explicitly mentions a 'sacred valley' (wādī ḥarām), indicating a pre-Islamic sacred space where certain activities like hunting were prohibited.
This verse contains an explicit oath 'By God' (wa-tā-llāhi), which is a common pre-Islamic oath formula.
This verse describes a scene using the simile of 'sacrificial camels of Iyad on the day their throats were slit,' explicitly referencing a pre-Islamic ritual slaughter.
This verse contains an explicit oath 'By God, the Glorious' (bi-llāhi l-majīd), reinforcing the use of pre-Islamic oath formulae.
The term 'dahr' (time/fate) is invoked as a cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic concept representing an impersonal destiny.
'Al-Munā' (fates/destinies) refers to pre-Islamic concepts of fate as an active cosmological agent, similar to dahr.
The poet addresses 'dahr' (my fate/time) as a personified cosmological force, reflecting pre-Islamic beliefs about destiny.
'Manā' (منى) is an explicit reference to Manāt, a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian goddess.
This verse explicitly mentions 'the veiled House' (al-Bayt al-Muḥajjab), which is a clear reference to the Kaaba, a central pre-Islamic sanctuary and sacred space.
